serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 240 g (one cup), providing about 101 kcal, 24.9 g carbohydrate, and 23.9 g sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Hot cocoa mix, prepared with water — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for hot chocolate / cocoa beverage as reported in the input record (cr_id G00010973, data_source hpb, confidence 0.95). The input per_100g values are preserved as-is from the HPB canonical record.

gi_source.title: Cocoa-based sweetened beverage — analogous estimate based on hot chocolate mix GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry exists for 'Premium Hot Chocolate' in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated based on the closest analogous entry: sweetened cocoa/hot chocolate mix beverages, which the University of Sydney GI Database reports in the range of 51–58 for sugar-containing cocoa drinks. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (24.86 g for 240 g serving) / 100 = 13.7. A GI of 55 falls into the 'low' category (GI <= 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (9.94 g/100 g) with negligible potassium (5 mg/100 g) and no dietary fibre; sugar-sweetened beverages independently elevate blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened cocoa beverage; request an unsweetened cocoa preparation with low-fat milk and no added sugar, as the AHA (2018) identifies sugar-sweetened beverages as an independent risk factor for elevated blood pressure regardless of sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 9.94 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre and a low GI of 55 makes this a high-concern sugar-sweetened beverage; liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage; the combination of 9.94 g sugar/100 g, zero dietary fibre, and liquid form results in rapid glycaemic response — an unsweetened cocoa powder preparation with low-fat milk and no added sugar is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar (9.94 g/100 g) with negligible fibre and potassium; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, weight gain, and CVD risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened hot chocolate; habitual intake of sugar-sweetened beverages raises triglycerides and promotes adiposity — unsweetened dark cocoa in low-fat milk without added sugar provides flavanol benefit without the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (9.94 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators and negates the potential anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit of cocoa.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request an unsweetened cocoa preparation; the high added sugar in this beverage promotes inflammatory pathways and offsets any anti-inflammatory benefit from cocoa flavanols.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Premium Hot Chocolate is a sweetened cocoa-based hot beverage prepared from cocoa powder, creamy milk, sugar, and sweet marshmallows. It is served hot and is popular in Western café and home settings, particularly as a comforting cold-weather drink. The beverage has a rich, sweet chocolate flavour with a creamy texture and a light marshmallow topping that melts into the drink. Per 100 g, it provides 42 kcal, 10.36 g carbohydrate (of which 9.94 g is sugar), 0.25 g fat, and negligible protein and dietary fibre. The very high sugar-to-fibre ratio and liquid form make this a high-glycaemic-impact beverage despite its low GI of 55, because liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its sugar-sweetened beverage profile. Sodium is very low at 3 mg/100 g. Cocoa naturally contains flavanols with anti-inflammatory properties, but the high added sugar in this preparation negates that benefit. An unsweetened cocoa preparation with low-fat milk and no added sugar would be a clinically appropriate alternative for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: Is hot chocolate high in sugar?
Is hot chocolate good for blood pressure?
Is cocoa drink suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010973, data_source hpb, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). The input record is a canonical HPB entry with provenance marked as canonical. All per_100g values are preserved from the approved primary source.
notes: protein_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database for this prepared hot chocolate beverage formulation — the beverage is predominantly sugar-water with cocoa and minimal milk solids at this dilution, resulting in protein below detection threshold; confirmed zero by HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — cocoa solids at this dilution contribute negligible fibre. magnesium_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this beverage. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this beverage as of 2026. saturated_fat_g of 0.15 g/100 g is estimated from the total fat of 0.25 g/100 g using a typical cocoa-milk beverage saturated fat proportion (~60% of total fat); HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately for this entry. Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples / Baking ingredients' to 'Beverages / Hot chocolate and cocoa drinks' as this is a prepared hot beverage, not a baking ingredient. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for sweetened cocoa/hot chocolate mix beverages (range 51–58); no exact entry exists for this specific product. GI of 55 is classified as 'low' (GI <= 55).
